statement.focus_question: Let $F(N)$ be the size of the largest subset of $\{1,\ldots,N\}$ which does not contain any set of the form $\{n,2n,3n\}$. What is\[ \lim_{N\to \infty}\frac{F(N)}{N}?
statement.normalized: Let $F(N)$ be the size of the largest subset of $\{1,\ldots,N\}$ which does not contain any set of the form $\{n,2n,3n\}$. What is\[ \lim_{N\to \infty}\frac{F(N)}{N}?\]Is this limit irrational?
statement.original: Let $F(N)$ be the size of the largest subset of $\{1,\ldots,N\}$ which does not contain any set of the form $\{n,2n,3n\}$. What is\[ \lim_{N\to \infty}\frac{F(N)}{N}?\]Is this limit irrational?
